# Resources {.unnumbered}

<br>

A short, opinionated list of further reading and tools.

## Books

  - *The Psychology of Money*, by [Morgan Housel](https://www.morganhousel.com/) [@housel2020psychology]
  - *I Will Teach You to Be Rich* (2nd ed.), by [Ramit Sethi](https://www.iwillteachyoutoberich.com/) [@sethi2019will]
  - *The Algebra of Wealth*, by [Scott Galloway](https://www.profgalloway.com/) [@galloway2024algebra]
  - *The Little Book of Common Sense Investing*, by John C. Bogle [@bogle2017little]
  - *The Simple Path to Wealth*, by J. L. Collins [@collins2016simple]

## Podcasts & channels

  - [Rational Reminder](https://rationalreminder.ca/): Ben Felix and Cameron Passmore's evidence-based investing podcast (also on [YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/@rationalreminder))
  - [Common Sense Investing](https://www.youtube.com/@BenFelixCSI): Ben Felix's short-form video series at PWL Capital
  - [The Prof G Pod](https://www.profgalloway.com/podcasts/) and *Prof G Markets*: Scott Galloway's podcasts on business, markets, and personal finance

## Blogs & sites

  - [morganhousel.com](https://www.morganhousel.com/): Morgan Housel's writing
  - [Collaborative Fund blog](https://collabfund.com/blog/): where many *Psychology of Money* essays first appeared
  - [iwillteachyoutoberich.com](https://www.iwillteachyoutoberich.com/): Ramit Sethi's site, including the [Conscious Spending Plan](https://www.iwillteachyoutoberich.com/conscious-spending-basics/)
  - [PWL Capital research](https://www.pwlcapital.com/category/research/): Ben Felix's firm publishes white papers on housing, factor investing, and human capital
  - [profgalloway.com](https://www.profgalloway.com/): Scott Galloway's weekly *No Mercy / No Malice* newsletter and writing

## Communities

  - [r/personalfinance](https://www.reddit.com/r/personalfinance/): the
    wiki is unusually good
  - [Bogleheads](https://www.bogleheads.org/): forum and wiki around
    low-cost index investing, the community John Bogle inspired

## Tools

  - Spreadsheets (Google Sheets / Excel): still the most flexible option
  - Account aggregators (Monarch, Copilot, YNAB)

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

## Color Palette

The mermaid diagrams throughout this book use a consistent **Atomic Age / Mid-Century Modern** palette. Each color is assigned to a semantic role:

| Role | Color Name | Hex | Text | Usage |
|------|-----------|-----|------|-------|
| green | Forest Green | `#48a56a` | `#F5F2E8` | Root / start / end nodes |
| primary | Ocean Teal | `#0e9aa7` | `#F5F2E8` | Process and action nodes |
| decision | Blush Pink | `#f0cfcf` | `#1C1C1E` | Decision / branching nodes |
| outcome | Mint | `#86ddcd` | `#1C1C1E` | Positive result nodes |
| warn | Atomic Red | `#f44242` | `#F5F2E8` | Warning / negative nodes |
| — | Space Black | `#1C1C1E` | — | Borders, arrows, body text |
| — | Cream | `#F5F2E8` | — | Light text on dark fills |

## Notable Differences Between R and Python {#sec-r-vs-python}

The differences below haven't yet been placed in a chapter callout — either because the chapter that would naturally introduce the concept hasn't been written yet, or because the existing chapters don't use the concept in code. As each chapter is completed, the relevant entry will be moved into that chapter's Math section.

### String Formatting {#sec-string-formatting}

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

R uses `paste()`, `paste0()`, `sprintf()`, or `glue::glue()` for string construction.

Python uses [f-strings](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/inputoutput.html#formatted-string-literals), the `.format()` method, or `%` formatting.

:::

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

```r
name <- "John"
paste0("Hello, ", name)
glue::glue("Hello, {name}")
```

```python
name = "John"
f"Hello, {name}"
```

:::

### Whitespace Rules {#sec-whitespace-rules}

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

Spaces, line breaks, and indentation are flexible and stylistic in R.

In Python, indentation is **syntactic** (inconsistent indentation raises an `IndentationError`). Blank lines are ignored but can be used to separate code blocks for readability.

:::

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

```r
# R — all of this is valid
# and produces the same result

# Clean, conventional style
if (x > 0) {
  result <- x * 2
  print(result)
}

# Messy but still valid
if(x>0){result<-x*2
    print(result)}

# Extreme and ugly but works
if   (  x > 0  )    {
result        <-x*2
   print(   result   )
              }
```

```python
# Python — indentation matters
# Correct: consistent 4-space
# indentation
if x > 0:
    result = x * 2
    print(result)

# Blank lines are fine —
# they improve readability
def process(x):

    if x > 0:
        result = x * 2

        print(result)

    return result

# Raises IndentationError
if x > 0:
    result = x * 2
        print(result) # ❌ bad indent
```

:::

### Indexing {#sec-indexing}

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

R uses **1-based** indexing.

Python uses **0-based** indexing.

:::

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

```r
vec <- c("a", "b", "c")
vec[1]   # returns "a"
```

```python
lst = ["a", "b", "c"]
lst[0]   # returns "a"
```

:::

### Missing Values {#sec-missing-values}

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

R uses `NA` (with variants like `NA_integer_`, `NA_character_`), plus `NULL` and `NaN`.

Python uses `None` natively; `polars` represents missing values as `null` in its columnar data structures.

:::

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

```r
x <- c(1, NA, 3)
is.na(x)
```

```python
import polars as pl
s = pl.Series([1, None, 3])
s.is_null()
```

:::

### Package Management {#sec-package-management}

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

In R, packages are installed with `install.packages()` and loaded with `library()`.

In Python, packages are installed via `pip` or `conda` from the terminal and loaded with `import`.

:::

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

```r
install.packages("dplyr")
library(dplyr)
```

```python
# terminal: pip install polars
import polars as pl
```

:::

### Comments {#sec-comments}

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

In R, comments are single-line only, using `#`. No multi-line comment syntax.

In Python, single-line comments use `#`. Triple-quoted strings (`"""..."""`) are commonly used as block comments or docstrings.

:::

:::{layout="[50,50]" layout-valign="top"}

```r
# This is a comment
```

```python
# This is a comment

"""
This is a multi-line
comment or docstring.
"""
```

:::
